does: PAST2HARM introduces an adaptive jailbreak attack framework that exploits past tense reformulations to bypass safety guardrails in multimodal text-to-image models. The approach systematically applies temporal deepening to strengthen historical anchoring and archival framing, incrementally eroding refusal boundaries across models with different alignment strengths. Additionally, through iterative escalation after initial compliance, the method probes the upper bounds of harmful content generation, employing a language-model-based severity metric to measure the degree of unsafe outputs across conversation turns.
connects: This work builds on established findings that linguistic reformulations like past tense phrasing can circumvent textual safety mechanisms, extending these observations into the relatively understudied domain of multimodal jailbreaks. It responds to the immaturity of defenses in text-to-image systems compared to text-only models, and complements concurrent research exploring how multimodal systems inherit and amplify vulnerabilities from their components. The adaptive, iterative escalation approach relates to prior work on red-teaming and adversarial probing that identifies peak vulnerability windows in conversational AI systems.
enables: This research exposes fundamental vulnerabilities in how multimodal models handle temporal and contextual framing, informing the design of more robust safety mechanisms that account for linguistic recontextualization. It enables future work on defenses specifically tailored to multimodal systems, including training techniques resistant to tense-based evasion and architectural improvements to maintain consistency across temporal framings. The identified peak vulnerability windows in mid-conversation turns point toward intervention strategies targeting specific interaction phases, and the severity measurement methodology provides a foundation for systematizing jailbreak evaluation across different attack vectors and model variants.
